title: US Strikes Iran Over Hormuz Tanker Attacks, Oil Rebounds as Ceasefire Frays
subhead: The US bombed Iranian targets after three commercial vessels were hit in the Strait of Hormuz, revoking an oil sanctions waiver and sending crude prices up 2%. Fed minutes under new Chair Warsh and Hong Kong's gold-clearing launch round out a volatile session.

sections.heading: US Strikes Iran After Tanker Attacks Shatter Hormuz Ceasefire
sections.body:
US Central Command launched what it called a 'series of powerful strikes' against Iran on Tuesday, hitting over 80 targets including more than 60 IRGC small boats, missile launch sites, and command centres. The strikes targeted Sirik, Qeshm island, and Bandar Abbas, according to Iranian state media, which reported civilians injured by shrapnel. The operation came in response to attacks on three commercial vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz: a Qatari LNG tanker (Al Rekayyat), a Saudi crude supertanker (Wedyan), and a third vessel struck by a drone. Qatar and Saudi Arabia both summoned Iranian diplomats, holding Tehran responsible.

The US Treasury simultaneously revoked the oil sanctions waiver it had granted Iran in June as part of the ceasefire memorandum. Obsidian Risk Advisors managing principal Brett Erickson told Nikkei that the waiver 'was one of the concessions Iran needed to justify lifting its blockade over the Strait of Hormuz.' Iran's foreign ministry called the revocation proof of US 'bad faith, inconsistency, and unreliability,' and warned it would 'take whatever measures it considers necessary.' Iran has not claimed responsibility for the tanker attacks.
sections.whyItMatters: The ceasefire that reopened Hormuz last month is now in jeopardy. If the waiver revocation sticks, Iran has little incentive to keep the strait open, and the 'start-stop nature' of Hormuz transits will continue injecting volatility into energy markets.

sections.heading: Oil Rebounds as Hormuz Risk Returns to 'Severe'
sections.body:
The US Navy-led Joint Maritime Information Center raised the threat level for Hormuz transits to 'severe' — the highest level since June 15 — citing 'deliberate hostile action likely under current conditions.' Traffic through the strait fell to about 16 vessels on Tuesday, the lowest in nearly three weeks, compared with an average of 25-40 daily over the past week and 125 before the conflict. Only about one-third to one-fifth of prewar traffic levels have resumed since the June ceasefire.

West Texas Intermediate futures for August delivery rose 2.1% to $71.87 per barrel in Asia trading, while Brent crude for September jumped 1.9% to $75.53. In postmarket activity, oil was up nearly 6% with Brent nearing $76. Average daily rates to load a ship inside the Gulf reached almost $300,000 a day, up from below $200,000 last week. Rapidan Energy Group president Bob McNally said the attacks and waiver revocation 'signal that the ceasefire is not as solid and durable as the oil market has chosen to assume.'
sections.whyItMatters: The market had priced in a gradual normalisation of Hormuz traffic. That assumption is now being challenged. Shipping rates doubling and the 'severe' risk rating suggest a return to war-period disruption levels, with knock-on effects for Asian importers who depend on Gulf crude.

sections.heading: Fed Minutes Loom as Warsh Era Begins With Hawkish Expectations
sections.body:
Investors are focused on Wednesday's release of FOMC minutes from Chair Kevin Warsh's first meeting, where officials left rates unchanged while signalling that additional hikes could be warranted if inflation persists. Vital Knowledge founder Adam Crisafulli called the minutes a 'wildcard,' noting that 'Warsh was so opaque at the most recent press conference' compared with his predecessor Jerome Powell, so 'the minutes, which are likely to be hawkish in tone, could contain some surprises.'

US equity futures were near flat Tuesday evening as investors weighed the Middle East escalation against the upcoming minutes. The Dow had fallen more than 100 points earlier Tuesday after touching a fresh intraday record, while the S&P 500 slipped 0.5% and the Nasdaq dropped 1.2% as chipmakers led declines — a rotation out of AI-linked names. The dollar slid on weaker-than-expected job growth, per BBC Business.
sections.whyItMatters: Warsh's opaque debut means the minutes are the first real window into how the new Fed leadership is thinking. Any hawkish surprises could clash with deteriorating risk sentiment from the Middle East, creating a policy-markets crosscurrent.

sections.heading: Asia-Pacific Markets Slide on Middle East Tensions
sections.body:
Asia-Pacific markets opened lower on Wednesday as US-Iran escalation weighed on sentiment. Japan's Nikkei 225 fell 0.55% and the Topix declined 0.7%. South Korea's Kospi dropped 0.72% and the Kosdaq fell 1.94%. Australia's S&P/ASX 200 was 1.36% lower. Hong Kong's Hang Seng bucked the trend, rising 1.2% on open, while mainland China's CSI 300 fell 0.47%.

South Korean defense stocks sold off sharply: Hanwha Aerospace dropped over 6% after losing a Canadian submarine contract to Germany's Thyssenkrupp Marine Systems, while Hyundai Rotem fell 9.23%. JPMorgan's chief Asia market strategist Tai Hui told CNBC that China's macroeconomic backdrop remains 'challenging' with weak retail sales and soft consumer sentiment, and the Hang Seng's underperformance reflects heavy exposure to e-commerce platforms 'getting squeezed' while spending on AI integration.
sections.whyItMatters: The divergence between risk-off in most Asian markets and Hang Seng's green open highlights a split: China's AI and tech sector is attracting capital even as traditional sectors face headwinds, and defense stocks are repricing as procurement decisions shift globally.

sections.heading: China Opens Lithium Futures to Foreign Traders, Cements Pricing Power
sections.body:
Beijing opened lithium futures to foreign traders, a move aimed at cementing China's dominance over global pricing for the battery metal. The Guangzhou Futures Exchange launched the international access as China — which processes roughly 65% of the world's lithium — seeks to formalise its pricing influence and attract overseas hedging activity. The push comes as China's EV sector faces headwinds: June EV deliveries fell 7% year-on-year to about 1 million vehicles, continuing a downward spiral that is exacerbating industry worries.

At the same time, SCMP reports that Chinese carmakers are 'rewriting Europe's auto hierarchy,' though the gains could slow if the EU widens tariffs on plug-in hybrids. The dual dynamic — pricing power in upstream commodities combined with downstream delivery softness — reflects the uneven nature of China's energy transition dominance.
sections.whyItMatters: China is building commodity pricing infrastructure (lithium futures, Hong Kong gold clearing) even as its end-demand falters. This upstream-downstream divergence matters: Beijing is locking in structural pricing power that will outlast any cyclical demand dip.

sections.heading: Hong Kong Launches Gold Clearing System as De-Dollarization Reshapes Reserves
sections.body:
Hong Kong on Tuesday launched a central clearing system for gold and revived gold futures trading, positioning itself as a regional reserve hub for the metal. The move competes directly with Singapore, which has also announced plans to set up gold clearing and storage systems. Nikkei Asia reports the initiative comes amid 'rising interest in gold and distrust of the dollar.'

Gold has fallen more than 20% since the Iran conflict began in late February, giving back much of the geopolitical premium that pushed prices to a record $5,354.80 settlement on January 29. August gold futures settled down 0.2% at $4,157.40 an ounce on Tuesday — gold's fourth straight losing session and its first negative day in four. The World Gold Council reports nearly 90% of central banks plan to increase gold holdings, reflecting a structural shift in reserve management away from the dollar.
sections.whyItMatters: Hong Kong and Singapore racing to build gold infrastructure is not just a market story — it is infrastructure for a multipolar reserve system. Even as gold prices pull back from wartime highs, the plumbing being built now will shape how central banks diversify for years.

sections.heading: Japanese Bond Yields March Toward 3%, China Debt Pressure Mounts
sections.body:
Japanese government bond yields are marching toward 3%, Nikkei Asia reports, as Prime Minister Takaichi's expansionary fiscal policy pushes up borrowing costs and worries grow over Bank of Japan tightening. The BOJ recently raised rates to a 31-year high and signalled readiness for further hikes to tame price pressures from the Iran-war energy shock. Meanwhile, yen weakness persists as carry trades and hedging deepen depreciation, with intervention offering only brief respite.

In China, Wuhan Z-Bank became the latest local bank absorbed by a state-directed entity due to 'severe credit risks,' Nikkei reports, highlighting the ongoing local government debt crunch. Foreign investors, however, have scooped up a half-year record $60 billion in Japanese stocks, drawn by AI and niche companies. The PBOC's Pan Gongsheng told a Hong Kong summit that Beijing would expand the annual quota for mainland Chinese investment in Hong Kong and Macao-traded bonds by 300 billion to 800 billion yuan ($44-118 billion).
sections.whyItMatters: The Asia-Pacific macro picture is bifurcating: Japan is tightening into fiscal expansion while China's local debt problems deepen. Capital is flowing toward Japan's reform story and away from China's structural risks — a trend that the Hormuz disruption may accelerate if it pushes the BOJ further into hawkish territory.

bottomLine: The US military response to the Hormuz tanker attacks and the revocation of Iran's oil waiver puts the June ceasefire at risk of unraveling. Oil has bounced 2-6% and shipping rates have surged, with the strait's 'severe' risk rating restoring war-period disruption levels. Fed minutes from Kevin Warsh's first meeting arrive Wednesday with the potential for hawkish surprises that could collide with a risk-off environment. Meanwhile, China is building commodity pricing infrastructure — lithium futures and Hong Kong gold clearing — that reflects long-term strategic positioning even as near-term demand softens. The Hormuz disruption, a hawkish Fed pivot, and Asian macro divergence all point to a volatile second half of July.
sources: BBC News, CNBC, Nikkei Asia, South China Morning Post, Reuters, Al Jazeera, The Guardian
